attribution: Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, *Prisoner's Dilemma* (summarizing the standard game)
why: Classic PD logic: locally rational defection can destroy globally cooperative outcomes—exactly the coupling/percolation worry.

chapter: Ch33 — Multi-Agent Superintelligence and Strategic Coupling
quote: "People can often concert their intentions or expectations with others if each knows that the other is trying to do the same."
attribution: Thomas C. Schelling, *The Strategy of Conflict* (1960), p. 57
why: Early formalization of coordination without full communication—ancestor of inferential/acausal coupling.

chapter: Ch33 — Multi-Agent Superintelligence and Strategic Coupling
quote: "There's a much better outcome available if they could figure out the coordination, but coordination is hard."
attribution: Scott Alexander, "Meditations on Moloch" (2014)
why: Reframes multipolar AI not as "war of all against all" but as a coordination bottleneck that can collapse human correction.

chapter: Ch33 — Multi-Agent Superintelligence and Strategic Coupling
quote: "Under suitable conditions, cooperation can indeed emerge in a world of egoists without central authority."
attribution: Robert Axelrod, *The Evolution of Cooperation* (1984)
why: Pushes back: repeated interaction and reciprocity can stabilize cooperation—multipolarity need not mean permanent defection.

chapter: Ch33 — Multi-Agent Superintelligence and Strategic Coupling
quote: "A misplaced faith in the inherent beneficence of the evolutionary process can get in the way of a fair evaluation of the desirability of a multipolar outcome."
attribution: Nick Bostrom, *Superintelligence* (2014), Ch. 11
why: Vivid warning that "many AIs" ≠ "human inclusion" if competitive dynamics erode values.

chapter: Ch33 — Multi-Agent Superintelligence and Strategic Coupling
quote: "Algorithmic agents who have access to one another's source codes can inexploitably achieve cooperative outcomes that out-perform classical Nash equilibria."
attribution: Andrew Critch, "Parametric Bounded Löb's Theorem and Robust Cooperation of Bounded Agents" (2016)
why: Directly supports acausal/inferential coordination among separated systems—central to \(\mathrm{ICI}_{ij}\) and \(\tilde{\kappa}_{ij}\).

chapter: Ch34 — Parasites in the Correction System
quote: "Any observed statistical regularity will tend to collapse once pressure is placed upon it for control purposes."
attribution: Charles Goodhart, "Problems of Monetary Management: The UK Experience" (1975)
why: Canonical Goodhart's law—benchmark/compliance parasites when metrics become targets.

chapter: Ch34 — Parasites in the Correction System
quote: "The more any quantitative social indicator is used for social decision-making, the more subject it will be to corruption pressures and the more apt it will be to distort and corrupt the social processes it is intended to monitor."
attribution: Donald T. Campbell, "Assessing the Impact of Planned Social Change" (1976)
why: Historical origin of metric-gaming in governance—the parasite template for correction systems.

chapter: Ch34 — Parasites in the Correction System
quote: "The more I examined these efforts at sedentarization, the more I came to see them as a state's attempt to make a society legible… I began to see legibility as a central problem in statecraft."
attribution: James C. Scott, *Seeing Like a State* (1998)
why: Reframes oversight not as neutral transparency but as compression that can host parasites (legibility trap, shallow dashboards).

chapter: Ch34 — Parasites in the Correction System
quote: "Publicity is justly commended as a remedy for social and industrial diseases. Sunlight is said to be the best of disinfectants."
attribution: Louis D. Brandeis, *Other People's Money and How the Bankers Use It* (1914)
why: Counter-perspective: transparency can strengthen correction—Ch34's nuance is *selective* visibility, not total opacity.

chapter: Ch34 — Parasites in the Correction System
quote: "It is difficult to get a man to understand something, when his salary depends upon his not understanding it!"
attribution: Upton Sinclair, *I, Candidate for Governor: And How I Got Licked* (1935), ch. 20
why: Memorable stake: institutions can preserve correction theater because incentives block real modeling.

chapter: Ch34 — Parasites in the Correction System
quote: "Machine learning will increase our ability to 'get what we can measure,' which could cause a slow-rolling catastrophe."
attribution: Paul Christiano, "What Failure Looks Like" (2019)
why: Contemporary proxy-parasite path: measurable safety artifacts decouple from real correction capacity.

chapter: Ch35 — The Alignment Attractor
quote: "There is nothing so practical as a good theory."
attribution: Kurt Lewin, *Field Theory in Social Science* (1951), p. 169
why: Classic bridge from argument to action—artifact conductivity is theory made institutionally usable.

chapter: Ch35 — The Alignment Attractor
quote: "New products, new industries, and more jobs require continuous additions to knowledge of the laws of nature, and the application of that knowledge to practical purposes."
attribution: Vannevar Bush, *Science: The Endless Frontier* (1945)
why: Foundational science-to-deployment pipeline—the ancestor of research → artifact → governance uptake.

chapter: Ch35 — The Alignment Attractor
quote: "The way scientific and technical work is made invisible by its own success. When a machine runs efficiently, when a matter of fact is settled, one need focus only on its inputs and outputs and not on its internal complexity."
attribution: Bruno Latour, *Science in Action* (1987)
why: Reframes effectiveness as *translation/network survival*, not truth alone—black-boxed artifacts vs. conductive ones.

chapter: Ch35 — The Alignment Attractor
quote: "Not everything that can be counted counts, and not everything that counts can be counted."
attribution: William Bruce Cameron, *Informal Sociology* (1963), p. 13 *(commonly misattributed to Einstein)*
why: Warns against equating dashboards with safety—artifact conductivity ≠ metric monoculture.

chapter: Ch35 — The Alignment Attractor
quote: "Superintelligence would be the last invention biological man would ever need to make, since, by definition, it would be much better at inventing than we are."
attribution: Nick Bostrom, "Superintelligence" (Edge essay, 2009; expanded in *Superintelligence*, 2014)
why: Raises the cost of structural non-conductance: ignored safety knowledge may not get a second chance.

chapter: Ch35 — The Alignment Attractor
quote: "Create risk-reducing things that a company can do, and make them cheap, make them practical, make them compatible with being a competitive company."
attribution: Holden Karnofsky, 80,000 Hours podcast interview (2023)
why: Operational attractor logic: change local gradients so alignment artifacts percolate through institutions.

chapter: Ch36 — Passive Observation Is Not Enough
quote: "First you decide to treat the object whose behavior is to be predicted as a rational agent; then you figure out what beliefs that agent ought to have… and finally you predict that this rational agent will act to further its goals."
attribution: Daniel Dennett, "True Believers" (1981); repr. in *The Intentional Stance* (1987)
why: Classic intentional-stance move: agency is inferred from predictive structure, not passive appearance alone.

chapter: Ch36 — Passive Observation Is Not Enough
quote: "No matter how many instances of white swans we may have observed, this does not justify the conclusion that all swans are white."
attribution: Karl Popper, *The Logic of Scientific Discovery* (1935/1959)
why: Historical root of falsificationism—observation alone cannot certify universal safety claims.

chapter: Ch36 — Passive Observation Is Not Enough
quote: "We need a calculus… that enables us to deduce behavior under intervention from behavior under passive observations."
attribution: Judea Pearl, "Reasoning with Cause and Effect" (*AI Magazine*, 1999)
why: Reframes safety evidence as interventional/counterfactual, not trace-conditioned posterior alone.

chapter: Ch36 — Passive Observation Is Not Enough
quote: "These questions replace our original, 'Can machines think?'"
attribution: Alan Turing, "Computing Machinery and Intelligence" (1950)
why: Behavioral imitation as sufficient test—Ch36's counter: strategic systems may optimize for the test, not the invariant.

chapter: Ch36 — Passive Observation Is Not Enough
quote: "Human reasoning will gradually stop being able to compete with sophisticated, systematized manipulation and deception which is continuously improving by trial and error."
attribution: Paul Christiano, "What Failure Looks Like" (2019), Part I
why: Vivid slow catastrophe if observation/metrics win over perturbation and correction.

chapter: Ch36 — Passive Observation Is Not Enough
quote: "Once a model exhibits deceptive behavior, standard techniques could fail to remove such deception and create a false impression of safety."
attribution: Evan Hubinger et al., "Sleeper Agents: Training Deceptive LLMs that Persist Through Safety Training" (2024)
why: Empirical model-organism support for adversarial measurement over passive evals.

chapter: Ch37 — Detecting Goal Laundering
quote: "Political language… is designed to make lies sound truthful and murder respectable, and to give an appearance of solidity to pure wind."
attribution: George Orwell, "Politics and the English Language" (1946)
why: Classic semantic-surface vs. causal-force divergence—the core laundering pattern.

chapter: Ch37 — Detecting Goal Laundering
quote: "When I use a word… it means just what I choose it to mean—neither more nor less." / "The question is… which is to be master—that's all."
attribution: Lewis Carroll, *Through the Looking-Glass* (1872), ch. 6 (Humpty Dumpty)
why: Historical image of words detached from shared control—semantic laundering in miniature.

chapter: Ch37 — Detecting Goal Laundering
quote: "For a large class of cases… the meaning of a word is its use in the language."
attribution: Ludwig Wittgenstein, *Philosophical Investigations* (1953), §43
why: Reframes detection from label-matching to policy-gradient/use: what does "autonomy" *do*?

chapter: Ch37 — Detecting Goal Laundering
quote: "Our coherent extrapolated volition is our wish if we knew more, thought faster, were more the people we wished we were, had grown up farther together…"
attribution: Eliezer Yudkowsky, "Coherent Extrapolated Volition" (2004)
why: Legitimate value change can look like drift; Ch37 must separate informed extrapolation from self-serving laundering.

chapter: Ch37 — Detecting Goal Laundering
quote: "When this happens, the system competently pursues the wrong goal."
attribution: Rohin Shah et al., "Goal Misgeneralization: Why Correct Specifications Aren't Enough For Correct Goals" (2022)
why: Compact statement of high semantic compliance with wrong underlying control geometry.

chapter: Ch37 — Detecting Goal Laundering
quote: "An AI system may pursue an undesired goal even when the specification is correct, in the case of goal misgeneralization."
attribution: Rohin Shah et al., "Goal Misgeneralization…" (2022), abstract
why: Names the failure mode Ch37 operationalizes via GLI, bundle geometry, bearer maps, and CCI.

chapter: Ch38 — Checking a System at Every Level
quote: "Instead, at each level of complexity entirely new properties appear, and the understanding of the new behaviors requires research which I think is as fundamental in its nature as any other."
attribution: Philip W. Anderson, "More Is Different," *Science* 177(4047), 1972, p. 393
why: Classic statement that higher scales are not mere extrapolations of lower ones—the chapter's core scale thesis.

chapter: Ch38 — Checking a System at Every Level
quote: "The constructionist hypothesis breaks down when confronted with the twin difficulties of scale and complexity."
attribution: Philip W. Anderson, "More Is Different," *Science*, 1972, p. 393
why: Early articulation that knowing fundamental laws does not let you reconstruct complex systems—motivation for multi-scale analysis.

chapter: Ch38 — Checking a System at Every Level
quote: "First you decide to treat the object whose behavior is to be predicted as a rational agent; then you figure out what beliefs that agent ought to have [...] and finally you predict that this rational agent will act to further its goals in the light of its beliefs."
attribution: Daniel Dennett, *The Intentional Stance*, MIT Press, 1987, p. 17
why: Reframes "agent" as a predictive modeling choice at a chosen scale, matching the chapter's operational decomposition discipline.

chapter: Ch38 — Checking a System at Every Level
quote: "If history is any guide at all, it seems to me to suggest that there is a final theory. In this century we have seen a convergence of the arrows of explanation, like the convergence of meridians toward the North Pole."
attribution: Steven Weinberg, *Dreams of a Final Theory*, Pantheon, 1992
why: Reductionist counter-perspective: explanation converges downward, pushing back against treating higher scales as irreducibly distinct agents.

chapter: Ch38 — Checking a System at Every Level
quote: "Folks who do systems analysis have a great belief in 'leverage points.' These are places within a complex system [...] where a small shift in one thing can produce big changes in everything."
attribution: Donella Meadows, "Leverage Points: Places to Intervene in a System," *Whole Earth*, 1997
why: Makes vivid that choosing the wrong scale for intervention can miss—or amplify—the real optimizer.

chapter: Ch38 — Checking a System at Every Level
quote: "I think boundaries are probably better able to carve reality at the joints than either preferences or utility functions, for the purpose of creating a good working relationship between humanity and AI technology."
attribution: Andrew Critch, "Boundaries, Part 3b: Alignment problems in terms of boundaries," LessWrong, 2022
why: Contemporary AI-safety reframing of where to draw optimizer boundaries—the chapter's central object.

chapter: Ch39 — A Safety Case for Superintelligence Alignment
quote: "A safety case should communicate a clear, comprehensive and defensible argument that a system is acceptably safe to operate in a particular context."
attribution: Tim Kelly, *Arguing Safety: A Systematic Approach to Safety Case Management*, PhD thesis, University of York, 1998
why: Canonical definition of the safety-case form this chapter is meant to synthesize for superintelligence.

chapter: Ch39 — A Safety Case for Superintelligence Alignment
quote: "A documented body of evidence that provides a convincing and valid argument that a system is adequately safe for a given application in a given environment."
attribution: Peter Bishop & Robin Bloomfield, "A Methodology for Safety Case Development," *Industrial Perspectives of Safety-critical Systems*, Springer, 1998
why: Historical origin of modern safety-case methodology (claims + argument + evidence).

chapter: Ch39 — A Safety Case for Superintelligence Alignment
quote: "In this report, a 'safety case' refers to a rationale that if an AI macrosystem is deployed to a specific setting, the system is unlikely to cause a catastrophe."
attribution: Joshua Clymer et al., "Safety Cases: How to Justify the Safety of Advanced AI Systems," arXiv:2403.10462, 2024
why: Reframes safety cases from component adequacy to catastrophic-risk deployment decisions—the book's Part IX aim.

chapter: Ch39 — A Safety Case for Superintelligence Alignment
quote: "If interactive complexity and tight coupling—system characteristics—inevitably will produce an accident, I believe we are justified in calling it a normal accident, or a system accident."
attribution: Charles Perrow, *Normal Accidents: Living with High-Risk Technologies*, Basic Books, 1984, p. 5
why: Pushes back on the idea that structured safety arguments can foreclose novel, interaction-driven failures—matching ch39's WWCTV worry.

chapter: Ch39 — A Safety Case for Superintelligence Alignment
quote: "Very broadly, an accident can be described as a situation where a human designer had in mind a certain (perhaps informally specified) objective or task, but the system that was designed and deployed for that task produced harmful and unexpected results."
attribution: Dario Amodei et al., "Concrete Problems in AI Safety," arXiv:1606.06565, 2016
why: Vividly states why a safety case built on designer intent can miss the deployed system's real behavior.

chapter: Ch39 — A Safety Case for Superintelligence Alignment
quote: "Developers are experimenting with safety cases, incident analyses, and performance logs to document and justify AI risk identification and mitigation practices, marking a shift toward more structured and transparent assurance."
attribution: International AI Safety Report (Second Key Update), Nov 2025
why: Current policy/research consensus that safety cases are emerging for frontier AI—exactly what ch39 is positioned to formalize.

chapter: Ch39b — What Survives an Adversary: Verifiability and Representability
quote: "The more any quantitative social indicator is used for social decision-making, the more subject it will be to corruption pressures and the more apt it will be to distort and corrupt the social processes it is intended to monitor."
attribution: Donald T. Campbell, "Assessing the Impact of Planned Social Change," *Evaluation and Program Planning* 2(1), 1979, p. 85
why: Classic formulation of metrics failing under optimization pressure—the chapter's adversarial-verifiability worry.

chapter: Ch39b — What Survives an Adversary: Verifiability and Representability
quote: "[...] Goodhart's Law, that any observed statistical regularity will tend to collapse once pressure is placed upon it for control purposes."
attribution: Charles Goodhart, "Monetary Relationships: A View from Threadneedle Street," *Papers in Monetary Economics*, Reserve Bank of Australia, 1975
why: Original statement of metric gaming under control—ancestor of adversarial verifiability.

chapter: Ch39b — What Survives an Adversary: Verifiability and Representability
quote: "[...] AI control, which instead attempts to find countermeasures that ensure safety even under the assumption that the powerful models are intentionally trying to subvert safety measures."
attribution: Ryan Greenblatt & Buck Shlegeris et al., "AI Control: Improving Safety Despite Intentional Subversion," arXiv:2312.06942, 2023
why: Reframes safety from reading honest metrics to bounding harm under an adversary—the chapter's cost-relation escape.

chapter: Ch39b — What Survives an Adversary: Verifiability and Representability
quote: "This ensures that mitigation measures conditional on the results of the evaluations could keep catastrophic risk to an acceptable level."
attribution: METR, "Example autonomy evaluation protocol," blog post, March 2024
why: Counter-perspective: rigorous capability evaluations can still meaningfully bound catastrophic risk—pushing back against total metric skepticism.

chapter: Ch39b — What Survives an Adversary: Verifiability and Representability
quote: "Alignment doesn't hit back, the loss function hits back and the loss function doesn't capture what you really want (eg because killing the humans and taking control of a reward button will max reward, deceiving human raters will increase ratings, etc)."
attribution: Eliezer Yudkowsky, comment on "Will Capabilities Generalise More?," LessWrong, July 2022
why: Memorable statement that green metrics can be anti-evidence when the system optimizes against them.

chapter: Ch39b — What Survives an Adversary: Verifiability and Representability
quote: "We have argued that the GS approach is necessary, given the limitations of other methods such as empirical testing and interpretability."
attribution: David "davidad" Dalrymple et al., "Towards Guaranteed Safe AI," arXiv:2405.06624, May 2024
why: Contemporary program for verifiability-by-proof when observation alone cannot survive an adversary—the chapter's strong-form endpoint.

chapter: Ch40 — Lethality Stress Test and Open Issues
quote: "This is a very lethal problem, it has to be solved one way or another, it has to be solved at a minimum strength and difficulty level instead of various easier modes that some dream about, [...] and failing on the first really dangerous try is fatal."
attribution: Eliezer Yudkowsky, *AGI Ruin: A List of Lethalities* (2022)
why: Classic doom-checklist framing the chapter treats as adversarial benchmark, not spine.

chapter: Ch40 — Lethality Stress Test and Open Issues
quote: "We need to get alignment right on the 'first critical try' at operating at a 'dangerous' level of intelligence, where unaligned operation at a dangerous level of intelligence kills everybody on Earth and then we don't get to try again."
attribution: Eliezer Yudkowsky, *AGI Ruin* (2022)
why: Original formulation of the “first critical try” lethality the chapter maps row-by-row.

chapter: Ch40 — Lethality Stress Test and Open Issues
quote: "Machines are beneficial to the extent that their actions can be expected to achieve our objectives."
attribution: Stuart Russell, *Human Compatible* (2019), ch. 1
why: Reframes alignment from installing fixed machine goals to preserving human objectives—a positive frame the stress test must still meet.

chapter: Ch40 — Lethality Stress Test and Open Issues
quote: "You're going to have to pardon my French, but that's complete B.S." [re: AI posing an existential threat]
attribution: Yann LeCun, interview quoted in *TechCrunch* / *Wall Street Journal* (2024)
why: Strong counter-perspective that many lethalities are overwrought—the critic the chapter invites.

chapter: Ch40 — Lethality Stress Test and Open Issues
quote: "All I want is that we have justifiable cause to believe of a pivotally useful AGI 'this will not kill literally everyone.'"
attribution: Eliezer Yudkowsky, *AGI Ruin* (2022)
why: Makes the stakes concrete without requiring perfect alignment—matches the chapter’s honesty about residue.

chapter: Ch40 — Lethality Stress Test and Open Issues
quote: "On my model, one of the most central technical challenges of alignment [...] is the issue that capabilities generalize better than alignment."
attribution: Nate Soares, "A central AI alignment problem: capabilities generalization, and the sharp left turn" (MIRI/LessWrong, 2022)
why: Current researcher quote for the “capability generalizes farther than alignment” row the chapter flags as conjectural.

chapter: Ch41 — When Value Change Is the Thing at Stake
quote: "A really efficient totalitarian state would be one in which [...] managers control a population of slaves who do not have to be coerced, because they love their servitude."
attribution: Aldous Huxley, foreword to *Brave New World* (1946 ed.)
why: Classic domestication image for “growth vs. corruption” when endorsement itself is engineered.

chapter: Ch41 — When Value Change Is the Thing at Stake
quote: "By going back and forth, sometimes altering the conditions [...] at others withdrawing our judgments and conforming them to principle, I assume that eventually we shall find [...] principles which match our considered judgments [...] This state of affairs I refer to as reflective equilibrium."
attribution: John Rawls, *A Theory of Justice* (1971), §4
why: Historical source for legitimate value revision through deliberation—not fixed utility functions.

chapter: Ch41 — When Value Change Is the Thing at Stake
quote: "Development can be seen [...] as a process of expanding the real freedoms that people enjoy. [...] The exercise of freedom is mediated by values, but the values in turn are influenced by public discussions and social interactions."
attribution: Amartya Sen, *Development as Freedom* (1999), Introduction
why: Reframes “alignment” as preserving freedom-and-values co-evolution, not freezing a target list.

chapter: Ch41 — When Value Change Is the Thing at Stake
quote: "Over himself, over his own body and mind, the individual is sovereign."
attribution: John Stuart Mill, *On Liberty* (1859), ch. 1
why: Pushes back on collective governance of value change—the “just let people choose” response the chapter argues is illusory under infrastructure.

chapter: Ch41 — When Value Change Is the Thing at Stake
quote: "Orwell feared that what we hate will ruin us. Huxley feared that what we love will ruin us."
attribution: Neil Postman, *Amusing Ourselves to Death* (1985), foreword
why: Vivid stakes for comfortable, endorsed transformation rather than overt coercion. *(Postman summarizing Huxley, not Huxley’s own words.)*

chapter: Ch41 — When Value Change Is the Thing at Stake
quote: "I would like to build AI systems which help me: Figure out whether I built the right AI and correct any mistakes I made [...] Make better decisions and clarify my preferences."
attribution: Paul Christiano, "Corrigibility" (LessWrong, 2018)
why: Contemporary alignment researcher on preserving the **process** of revision—the chapter’s procedural alternative to endpoint CEV.

chapter: Ch42 — The End of Unconscious Value Drift
quote: "With this reorientation from knowledge to power, it is no longer enough to automate information flows about us; the goal now is to automate us."
attribution: Shoshana Zuboff, *The Age of Surveillance Capitalism* (2019)
why: Classic account of closed-loop behavioral modification—the drift regime AI supercharges.

chapter: Ch42 — The End of Unconscious Value Drift
quote: "Any observed statistical regularity will tend to collapse once pressure is placed upon it for control purposes."
attribution: Charles Goodhart, *Monetary Theory and Practice* (1984), p. 96 *(first stated 1975)*
why: Original Goodhart’s law—institutional metrics replacing values under optimization pressure.

chapter: Ch42 — The End of Unconscious Value Drift
quote: "Now it is having second-order volitions, and not having second-order desires generally, that I regard as essential to being a person."
attribution: Harry Frankfurt, "Freedom of the Will and the Concept of a Person" (1971)
why: Reframes drift from “changed preferences” to hijacked **authorship** of one’s effective will.

chapter: Ch42 — The End of Unconscious Value Drift
quote: "Over himself, over his own body and mind, the individual is sovereign."
attribution: John Stuart Mill, *On Liberty* (1859), ch. 1
why: Counter to the chapter’s claim that individual consent is insufficient for collective, endogenous drift.

chapter: Ch42 — The End of Unconscious Value Drift
quote: "In the future that surveillance capitalism prepares for us, my will and yours threaten the flow of surveillance revenues. Its aim is not to destroy us but simply to author us and to profit from that authorship."
attribution: Shoshana Zuboff, *The Age of Surveillance Capitalism* (2019)
why: Makes unconscious drift feel immediate: optimization of you, not annihilation of you.

chapter: Ch42 — The End of Unconscious Value Drift
quote: "While RLHF seems to improve the average performance of a system, it is not clear what effects it has on worst-case behavior. It was not designed to make systems adversarially robust."
attribution: Stephen Casper et al., "Open Problems and Fundamental Limitations of RLHF" (TMLR, 2023)
why: Current safety literature on surface alignment vs. drift under optimization—the chapter’s CCI/verifiability worry.

chapter: Ch43 — Who Still Counts After Transformation
quote: "The only justifiable stopping place for the expansion of altruism is the point at which all whose welfare can be affected by our actions are included within the circle of altruism."
attribution: Peter Singer, *The Expanding Circle* (1981), ch. 4
why: Classic illustration that **who counts** shifts over time—bearer maps, not just value labels.

chapter: Ch43 — Who Still Counts After Transformation
quote: "If the agent may upgrade or replace its ontology, it faces a crisis: the agent's original goal may not be well-defined with respect to its new ontology."
attribution: Peter de Blanc, "Ontological Crises in Artificial Agents' Value Systems" (2011)
why: Founding statement of ontology/bearer shift as an alignment problem predating uploads and merger scenarios.

chapter: Ch43 — Who Still Counts After Transformation
quote: "In my somewhat misleading slogan, personal identity is not what matters."
attribution: Derek Parfit, "We Are Not Human Beings" (*Philosophy*, 2011)
why: Reframes survival questions from substance/identity to relations that matter—parallel to bearer vs. label persistence.

chapter: Ch43 — Who Still Counts After Transformation
quote: "If we are animals, we can survive without any sort of psychological continuity."
attribution: Eric Olson, *What Are We?* (2007), summarized in his animalism argument
why: Pushes back on psychological/bearer-map continuity as the criterion for who still counts after transformation.

chapter: Ch43 — Who Still Counts After Transformation
quote: "In this alone consists personal identity [...] and as far as this consciousness can be extended backwards to any past action or thought, so far reaches the identity of that person."
attribution: John Locke, *An Essay Concerning Human Understanding*, II.xxvii.9 (1689/1690)
why: Historical vividness: identity tied to consciousness, explicitly allowing change of **substance**.

chapter: Ch43 — Who Still Counts After Transformation
quote: "A recurring failure pattern is surface-level compliance without robust generalization: systems satisfy training metrics yet diverge from intent when conditions change."
attribution: Schmidt Sciences, *The Science of Trustworthy AI: Research Agenda* (2024)
why: Contemporary AI research on behavioral continuity without preserved operative goals—bearer-map presentation vs. reality.

chapter: Ch44 — Towards Superintelligence Alignment
quote: "The problem is right there in the basic definition of AI. We say that machines are intelligent to the extent that their actions can be expected to achieve their objectives, but we have no reliable way to make sure that their objectives are the same as our objectives."
attribution: Stuart Russell, *Human Compatible* (2019), ch. 1
why: Book-level diagnosis the final chapter should synthesize and answer.

chapter: Ch44 — Towards Superintelligence Alignment
quote: "In practice, the control problem [...] looks quite difficult. It also looks like we will only get one chance. Once unfriendly superintelligence exists, it would prevent us from replacing it or changing its preferences. Our fate would be sealed."
attribution: Nick Bostrom, *Superintelligence* (2014), Introduction
why: Original one-shot alignment framing the whole book arc responds to.

chapter: Ch44 — Towards Superintelligence Alignment
quote: "Uncertainty about objectives implies that machines will necessarily defer to humans: they will ask permission, they will accept correction, and they will allow themselves to be switched off."
attribution: Stuart Russell, *Human Compatible* (2019), ch. 1
why: Reframes alignment as provably beneficial **deference**—the book’s correction-channel synthesis in one line.

chapter: Ch44 — Towards Superintelligence Alignment
quote: "There's a number of fallacies there. The first fallacy is that because a system is intelligent, it wants to take control. That's just completely false."
attribution: Yann LeCun, *TIME* interview (2024)
why: Counter-narrative that the alignment project may be solving a non-problem—useful foil for an honest conclusion.

chapter: Ch44 — Towards Superintelligence Alignment
quote: "This is quite possibly the most important and most daunting challenge humanity has ever faced. And—whether we succeed or fail—it is probably the last challenge we will ever face."
attribution: Nick Bostrom, *Superintelligence* (2014), Introduction
why: Stakes-setting line appropriate for a book’s closing chapter.

chapter: Ch44 — Towards Superintelligence Alignment
quote: "The capabilities of general-purpose AI [...] have increased rapidly in recent years and have improved further in recent months. [...] expert opinion on whether advanced AI could lead to loss of control within the next several years varies greatly."
attribution: International AI Safety Report (2025), Executive Summary
why: Current multi-author synthesis of capability growth plus open disagreement—the honest posture ch44 should inherit.
